title: Slab-built cup in air-dry clay
subtitle: A decorative cup with a slab body and applied handle
excerpt: A slab-built cup is a cylinder assembled from a rolled slab with a slab or coil handle. Air-dry clay is not food-safe, so this cup is a decorative object, for pencils, brushes, or dry kitchen tools. It uses score-and-slip joining at the seam and handle attachment, and is sealed once bone-dry. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Slab-built cylinder technique. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.
Scratching joining surfaces and applying clay slurry before pressing together, creating a strong mechanical bond.
The drying stage where the slab holds its cylindrical shape without support, the right time to attach the handle.

content.text: -built cup is a rectangle of clay rolled into a cylinder, with a base and a handle joined on using

. Air-dry clay is not food-safe: use the cup for pencils, brushes, small scissors, or dried flowers. It is a practical desk or kitchen object that uses the same slab and joining techniques as larger functional pottery.

attrs.title: Not for drinks
attrs.body: Air-dry clay is porous and not food-safe; use this cup for dry items only.

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 8 mm guide rails
Cardboard tube, 8 cm diameter (former for shaping)

content.text: Step 1: Roll and cut the wall slab

Roll a slab 8 mm thick. For a cup 8 cm in diameter and 10 cm tall, cut a rectangle 25 cm long (the circumference of an 8-cm tube) by 10 cm wide. The rectangle becomes the cup wall. Cut a circle 8 cm in diameter for the base. Leave both on the canvas board for one hour until slightly stiffer, still pliable but not floppy.

Wrap the rectangle around a cardboard tube covered with a single layer of baking paper (so it releases cleanly). The two short edges meet to form the vertical seam. Score both edges and apply slurry. Press the seam together firmly and smooth the interior of the join. Leave the cardboard former inside for 30 minutes until the cylinder holds its shape, then slide it out.

Score the bottom rim of the cylinder and the perimeter of the base circle. Apply slurry to both. Press the cylinder down onto the base circle and blend the interior seam with a wooden finger or the handle of the sponge. Reinforce with a thin coil of clay pressed into the interior corner and smoothed flat.
Step 4: Add the handle at leather-hard

, roll a coil 15 mm in diameter and 12 cm long. Curve it into a C-shape. Score two oval areas on the cup wall where the handle ends will attach. Score both ends of the handle. Apply slurry to all scored surfaces and press the handle firmly into position. Blend each attachment point with a small tool.
Dry bone-dry over two to three days. Sand rough spots with 120-grit sandpaper. Seal the exterior with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1. Paint with acrylics before sealing for colour.
Press a stamp or leaf into the wet slab wall before forming the cylinder.
Make a tapered cup by cutting a trapezoid rather than a rectangle, wider at the top.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when building the slab cup.

items.symptom: Vertical seam cracks open as the cup dries
items.cause: The seam was not scored and slipped on both faces, or was not blended on the interior.
items.fix: Score both crack surfaces, apply slurry, press together and blend the interior. Dry slowly under plastic for the first 24 hours.

items.symptom: Handle drops off before the cup is bone-dry
items.cause: Handle attached to a cup that was still too wet, or not scored thoroughly.
items.fix: Score four to six short hatched lines on the attachment point and the handle end, not just a light scratch. Press firmly and support the handle with a rolled-up cloth until it is firm.
